name: Pleural Effusion
signs:
Dyspnea
Pleuritic chest pain
Decreased breath sounds
Dullness to percussion
Decreased tactile fremitus
Tracheal deviation (large effusion)
Egophony at fluid level
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause
2. Diagnostic thoracentesis (new effusion)
3. Therapeutic thoracentesis if symptomatic (max 1-1.5L at a time)
4. Chest tube for empyema or complicated parapneumonic effusion
5. Pleurodesis for recurrent malignant effusion
6. Diuretics for transudative effusion (CHF)
meds:
Furosemide 40-80mg IV (if CHF-related)
Antibiotics if parapneumonic/empyema
Intrapleural tPA + dornase alfa (complicated effusion)
Pleurodesis agents: talc, doxycycline, bleomycin
ddx: CHF, Pneumonia, Malignancy, PE, Cirrhosis, Nephrotic syndrome, TB, Pancreatitis
labs:
CXR (blunting of costophrenic angle, >200mL needed)
Ultrasound (best for guiding thoracentesis)
Thoracentesis: Light's criteria (protein, LDH)
Cell count, glucose, pH, culture, cytology
CT chest if unclear etiology
notes:
Light's criteria for exudate (any 1): protein ratio >0.5, LDH ratio >0.6, LDH >2/3 upper normal
Transudate: CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome
Exudate: infection, malignancy, PE, autoimmune
Complicated parapneumonic: pH <7.2, glucose <60, positive culture → needs drainage
Massive effusion >2/3 hemithorax: think malignancy
description: Abnormal buildup of fluid between the layers of tissue lining the lungs and chest cavity, which can compress the lung and cause breathing difficulty.

name: Pulmonary Fibrosis
signs:
Progressive dyspnea
Dry nonproductive cough
Bibasilar inspiratory crackles (Velcro-like)
Digital clubbing
Cyanosis (late)
Rapid shallow breathing
Weight loss
treatment:
1. Antifibrotic therapy (pirfenidone or nintedanib) for IPF
2. Supplemental oxygen if hypoxemic
3. Pulmonary rehabilitation
4. Lung transplant evaluation for progressive disease
5. Treat underlying cause if not idiopathic
6. Avoid triggers/exposures
meds:
Pirfenidone 801mg TID with food
Nintedanib 150mg BID with food
Supplemental O2 to maintain SpO2 >88%
Proton pump inhibitor (GERD common comorbidity)
Avoid immunosuppression in IPF (PANTHER trial showed harm)
ddx: COPD, CHF, Hypersensitivity pneumonitis, Sarcoidosis, Asbestosis, Drug-induced lung disease, Connective tissue disease-associated ILD
labs:
PFTs (restrictive: reduced FVC, reduced DLCO, normal or increased FEV1/FVC ratio)
HRCT (UIP pattern: bibasilar honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, reticulation)
CXR (bilateral reticular opacities)
6-minute walk test
ABG
ANA, RF, anti-CCP (rule out CTD-ILD)
Surgical lung biopsy if HRCT atypical
notes:
IPF: most common idiopathic interstitial pneumonia, median survival 3-5 years
UIP pattern on HRCT: definite IPF diagnosis without biopsy (in right clinical context)
Acute exacerbation: rapid decline, bilateral GGO on CT, high mortality
Multidisciplinary discussion recommended for diagnosis
Pirfenidone and nintedanib slow FVC decline but don't cure
description: A chronic lung disease where the tissue deep in the lungs becomes thick, stiff, and scarred over time, making it progressively harder to breathe.

name: Sarcoidosis
signs:
Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy
Dyspnea and cough
Erythema nodosum
Fever and fatigue
Arthralgia
Uveitis
Skin plaques/papules
Cranial nerve palsies
Hepatomegaly
treatment:
1. Observation for mild disease (many spontaneously remit)
2. Systemic corticosteroids for significant organ involvement
3. Steroid-sparing agents for chronic disease
4. Topical steroids for skin/eye disease
5. Treat hypercalcemia if present
meds:
Prednisone 20-40mg daily x4-6 weeks then taper over 6-12 months
Methotrexate 10-15mg weekly (steroid-sparing)
Azathioprine 50-200mg daily
Hydroxychloroquine 200mg BID (skin/joint)
Infliximab 3-5mg/kg IV (refractory)
Mycophenolate 1-1.5g BID
ddx: Lymphoma, Tuberculosis, Berylliosis, Histoplasmosis, Hypersensitivity pneumonitis, HIV-associated lymphadenopathy
labs:
CXR (bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy)
CT chest
PFTs (restrictive or obstructive pattern)
ACE level (elevated in 60% but nonspecific)
Calcium (hypercalcemia in 10%)
24hr urine calcium
Biopsy: noncaseating granulomas
CBC, BMP, LFTs
Ophthalmologic exam
notes:
Staging: 0-no involvement, I-bilateral hilar LAD, II-LAD+infiltrates, III-infiltrates only, IV-fibrosis
Stage I: 60-80% spontaneous remission
Lofgren syndrome: erythema nodosum + bilateral hilar LAD + arthritis + fever (excellent prognosis)
African Americans: more severe, chronic disease
Cardiac sarcoid: arrhythmias, heart block - needs cardiac MRI
description: An inflammatory disease that causes clusters of immune cells (granulomas) to form in various organs, most commonly the lungs and lymph nodes.

name: Pulmonary Hypertension
signs:
Progressive dyspnea on exertion
Fatigue
Chest pain
Syncope with exertion
Loud P2 (pulmonic component of S2)
RV heave
JVD
Peripheral edema
Tricuspid regurgitation murmur
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause (Group 2: optimize HF, Group 3: treat lung disease)
2. PAH-specific therapy for Group 1 (vasodilators)
3. Diuretics for RV volume overload
4. Supplemental oxygen if hypoxic
5. Anticoagulation for CTEPH and idiopathic PAH
6. Pulmonary endarterectomy for CTEPH
7. Lung transplant for refractory disease
meds:
Ambrisentan 5-10mg daily (ERA)
Tadalafil 40mg daily (PDE5i)
Riociguat 1-2.5mg TID (sGC stimulator)
Epoprostenol continuous IV (severe PAH)
Treprostinil SC/IV/inhaled
Selexipag 200-1600mcg BID (oral prostacyclin)
Furosemide for volume management
ddx: Left heart disease, COPD/ILD, Chronic PE, Sleep apnea, Congenital heart disease, Sickle cell disease
labs:
TTE (elevated RVSP, RV dilation)
Right heart catheterization (mPAP >20mmHg at rest - diagnostic)
PFTs and DLCO
6-minute walk test
VQ scan (CTEPH)
BNP/NT-proBNP
CT angiography
HIV, ANA, LFTs (connective tissue screen)
Vasoreactivity testing
notes:
WHO Groups: 1-PAH, 2-left heart disease, 3-lung disease, 4-CTEPH, 5-multifactorial
Right heart cath is gold standard - mPAP >20mmHg defines PH
Vasoreactivity testing: positive → trial CCB (only in Group 1)
CTEPH is only surgically curable form (pulmonary endarterectomy)
Progressive RV failure is main cause of death
description: Abnormally high blood pressure in the arteries of the lungs, which forces the right side of the heart to work harder and can lead to heart failure.

name: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
signs:
Loud snoring
Witnessed apneas
Excessive daytime sleepiness
Morning headaches
Difficulty concentrating
Nocturia
Obesity
Large neck circumference (>17in men, >16in women)
Crowded oropharynx
treatment:
1. CPAP is first-line treatment
2. Weight loss (can be curative if mild)
3. Positional therapy (avoid supine sleeping)
4. Oral appliance (mandibular advancement device) for mild-moderate
5. Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP) if CPAP intolerant
6. Hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire device)
7. Avoid sedatives and alcohol before sleep
meds:
CPAP titration per sleep study
No pharmacologic treatment is first-line
Modafinil 200mg daily (residual sleepiness despite CPAP)
Topical nasal steroids (nasal congestion)
Avoid: benzodiazepines, opioids, alcohol at bedtime
ddx: Central sleep apnea, Narcolepsy, Hypothyroidism, Depression, Obesity hypoventilation syndrome, Periodic limb movement disorder
labs:
Polysomnography (gold standard) - AHI defines severity
Home sleep apnea test (HSAT) for uncomplicated cases
Epworth Sleepiness Scale
TSH (rule out hypothyroidism)
ABG if obesity hypoventilation suspected
CBC (polycythemia)
notes:
AHI: 5-15 mild, 15-30 moderate, >30 severe
Increased risk: HTN, AFib, stroke, MI, type 2 DM, MVA
CPAP compliance: goal >4hr/night on >70% of nights
Preoperative screening: STOP-BANG questionnaire
Refractory HTN: consider OSA as underlying cause
description: A sleep disorder where the throat muscles intermittently relax and block the airway during sleep, causing repeated breathing interruptions and poor sleep quality.

name: Lung Abscess
signs:
Productive cough (foul-smelling/putrid sputum)
Fever and night sweats
Weight loss
Pleuritic chest pain
Hemoptysis
Dental disease (aspiration risk)
Clubbing (chronic)
treatment:
1. Prolonged antibiotic therapy (6-8 weeks minimum)
2. Postural drainage
3. CT-guided percutaneous drainage if antibiotics fail
4. Bronchoscopy to rule out obstruction
5. Surgical resection for refractory cases
6. Dental evaluation and treatment
meds:
Clindamycin 600mg IV q8h then 300mg PO QID
Ampicillin/sulbactam 3g IV q6h
Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
Metronidazole 500mg IV/PO q8h (with penicillin)
Moxifloxacin 400mg daily (alternative)
ddx: Cavitary lung cancer, TB, Pulmonary infarction, Granulomatosis with polyangiitis, Septic emboli, Fungal infection
labs:
CXR (cavity with air-fluid level)
CT chest (better characterization)
Sputum culture and gram stain
Blood cultures
CBC (leukocytosis, anemia)
Bronchoscopy (rule out obstructing lesion)
notes:
Most common cause: aspiration of oropharyngeal contents (anaerobes)
Risk factors: alcoholism, poor dentition, dysphagia, seizures, altered consciousness
Primary: aspiration-related, usually right lower lobe or posterior segments
Secondary: post-obstructive (tumor), hematogenous (endocarditis)
Most respond to antibiotics alone; surgery <10% of cases
description: A localized area of pus and dead tissue within the lung, usually caused by bacterial infection from aspiration, forming a cavity visible on imaging.

name: Bronchiectasis
signs:
Chronic productive cough (large volume purulent sputum)
Recurrent respiratory infections
Hemoptysis
Dyspnea
Crackles and rhonchi
Clubbing
Wheezing
treatment:
1. Airway clearance techniques (chest physiotherapy, PEP devices)
2. Inhaled hypertonic saline for mucus mobilization
3. Antibiotics for acute exacerbations
4. Long-term macrolide therapy if frequent exacerbations (>=3/year)
5. Treat underlying cause
6. Surgical resection for localized disease with recurrent infections
7. Pulmonary rehabilitation
meds:
Azithromycin 250mg daily or 500mg 3x/week (maintenance)
Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875/125mg BID (exacerbation)
Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID (Pseudomonas)
Inhaled tobramycin 300mg BID (chronic Pseudomonas)
Hypertonic saline 7% nebulized BID
Albuterol 2.5mg nebulized PRN
ddx: COPD, Chronic bronchitis, Cystic fibrosis, Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, NTM infection, Primary ciliary dyskinesia
labs:
HRCT chest (dilated airways, signet ring sign, tram tracking)
Sputum culture and sensitivity
PFTs (obstructive pattern)
CBC, immunoglobulin levels
Alpha-1 antitrypsin level
Sweat chloride (CF)
Aspergillus IgE and IgG (ABPA)
Ciliary biopsy if PCD suspected
notes:
Vicious cycle: infection → inflammation → airway damage → impaired clearance → more infection
Common organisms: H. influenzae, Pseudomonas (poor prognosis), S. aureus, NTM
Causes: post-infectious, CF, immune deficiency, ABPA, autoimmune, alpha-1 AT deficiency
Pseudomonas colonization: associated with worse outcomes
Massive hemoptysis: bronchial artery embolization
description: Permanent widening and damage of the airways (bronchi) that leads to chronic cough with excessive mucus production and recurrent lung infections.

name: Tuberculosis
signs:
Chronic cough (>2-3 weeks)
Hemoptysis
Night sweats
Weight loss
Fever (low-grade)
Fatigue
Anorexia
Upper lobe cavitary lesions on CXR
treatment:
1. RIPE therapy for 2 months (intensive phase)
2. Isoniazid + rifampin for 4 months (continuation phase)
3. DOT (directly observed therapy) recommended
4. Pyridoxine (B6) with isoniazid to prevent neuropathy
5. Airborne isolation until 3 negative AFB smears
6. Contact tracing and public health notification
meds:
Isoniazid (INH) 5mg/kg daily (max 300mg)
Rifampin 10mg/kg daily (max 600mg)
Pyrazinamide 25mg/kg daily
Ethambutol 15mg/kg daily
Pyridoxine (B6) 25-50mg daily
Latent TB: INH x9 months or rifampin x4 months or INH+rifapentine x3 months (weekly)
ddx: Lung cancer, Pneumonia, Lung abscess, NTM infection, Sarcoidosis, Fungal infection (histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis)
labs:
CXR (upper lobe infiltrates, cavitation, miliary pattern)
AFB smear and culture x3 (sputum)
NAAT/GeneXpert (rapid molecular testing)
TST (Mantoux) or IGRA (QuantiFERON)
CT chest
HIV testing (all TB patients)
LFTs (baseline before treatment)
Drug susceptibility testing
notes:
Reportable disease - notify public health department
MDR-TB: resistant to INH + rifampin; XDR-TB: MDR + fluoroquinolone + injectable
Miliary TB: hematogenous dissemination, multiple organ involvement
Rifampin: potent CYP450 inducer - many drug interactions (OCP, warfarin, HIV meds)
INH side effects: hepatitis, peripheral neuropathy (prevent with B6)
Ethambutol: optic neuritis (check visual acuity monthly)
description: A bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis that primarily affects the lungs, spread through airborne droplets when an infected person coughs.

name: Aspiration Pneumonia
signs:
Cough with foul-smelling sputum
Fever
Dyspnea
Tachypnea
Crackles in dependent lung zones
Hypoxia
History of aspiration event or risk factors
Insidious onset
treatment:
1. Antibiotics covering oral anaerobes and gram-negatives
2. Supplemental oxygen
3. Aspiration precautions (HOB elevation, NPO if active aspiration)
4. Speech-language pathology swallow evaluation
5. Diet modification based on swallow study
6. Treat underlying cause of aspiration
meds:
Ampicillin/sulbactam 3g IV q6h
Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
Clindamycin 600mg IV q8h (if penicillin allergic)
Meropenem 1g IV q8h (if resistant organisms)
Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h + ceftriaxone 1g IV daily
ddx: Community-acquired pneumonia, Chemical pneumonitis, Lung abscess, PE, ARDS, Pulmonary edema
labs:
CXR (infiltrate in dependent segments - RLL, posterior segments)
CT chest if complications suspected
Sputum culture
Blood cultures
CBC, BMP, lactate
ABG if hypoxic
Swallow study (videofluoroscopy or FEES)
notes:
Dependent segments: supine = superior segments of lower lobes, posterior upper lobes; upright = basilar segments of lower lobes
Risk factors: altered consciousness, dysphagia, GERD, poor dentition, NG/OG tube, post-extubation
Chemical pneumonitis (Mendelson syndrome): sterile aspiration of gastric acid, no antibiotics initially
Aspiration of foreign body: may need bronchoscopy for removal
description: A lung infection that occurs when food, saliva, liquids, or vomit is inhaled into the lungs, commonly affecting people with swallowing difficulties or altered consciousness.

name: Respiratory Failure
signs:
Dyspnea and tachypnea
Use of accessory muscles
Cyanosis
Altered mental status
Diaphoresis
Tachycardia
Tripod positioning
Paradoxical abdominal breathing
Hypoxemia (SpO2 <90%)
treatment:
1. Supplemental oxygen (target SpO2 88-92% in COPD, 92-96% otherwise)
2. Noninvasive ventilation (BiPAP/CPAP) if appropriate
3. Endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation if NIV fails
4. Treat underlying cause
5. Lung-protective ventilation if ARDS (low tidal volume 6mL/kg IBW)
6. Prone positioning if severe ARDS
meds:
Supplemental O2 via NC, Venturi mask, NRB, or HFNC
Albuterol 2.5mg nebulized (bronchospasm)
Methylprednisolone 125mg IV (COPD/asthma exacerbation)
Antibiotics if infectious etiology
Sedation for intubated patients: propofol, fentanyl, dexmedetomidine
Neuromuscular blockade: cisatracurium (severe ARDS)
ddx: COPD exacerbation, Asthma, Pneumonia, PE, ARDS, CHF/pulmonary edema, Neuromuscular disease, Pneumothorax
labs:
ABG (Type 1: PaO2 <60mmHg; Type 2: PaCO2 >50mmHg)
Pulse oximetry
CXR
CBC, BMP, lactate
BNP (cardiac vs pulmonary)
Blood cultures if sepsis suspected
CT chest/CTA if PE suspected
notes:
Type 1 (hypoxemic): PaO2 <60mmHg on room air - VQ mismatch, shunt, diffusion impairment
Type 2 (hypercapnic): PaCO2 >50mmHg - hypoventilation, increased dead space
P/F ratio: PaO2/FiO2 - <300 = ARDS, <200 = moderate, <100 = severe
ARDSNet protocol: Vt 6mL/kg IBW, plateau pressure <30cmH2O
Avoid hyperoxia: target SpO2 92-96% (88-92% in COPD/CO2 retainers)
description: A life-threatening condition where the lungs cannot adequately oxygenate the blood or remove carbon dioxide, requiring urgent respiratory support.

name: Empyema
signs:
Fever persisting despite antibiotics for pneumonia
Pleuritic chest pain
Dyspnea
Decreased breath sounds
Dullness to percussion
Weight loss
Night sweats
Leukocytosis
treatment:
1. IV antibiotics
2. Chest tube drainage (large bore preferred)
3. Intrapleural fibrinolytics if loculated
4. VATS (video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery) for organized empyema
5. Open decortication for chronic organized empyema
6. Nutritional support
meds:
Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily + metronidazole 500mg IV q8h
Ampicillin/sulbactam 3g IV q6h
Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
Vancomycin 15-20mg/kg IV q8-12h (if MRSA risk)
Intrapleural tPA 10mg + dornase alfa 5mg BID via chest tube
ddx: Complicated parapneumonic effusion, Lung abscess, Malignant pleural effusion, TB pleuritis, Chylothorax
labs:
CT chest with contrast (enhanced pleural thickening, split pleura sign)
Thoracentesis: frank pus, pH <7.2, glucose <40, LDH >1000, positive Gram stain/culture
Blood cultures
CBC (leukocytosis)
CRP/ESR
Pleural fluid culture (anaerobes common)
notes:
Evolution: simple parapneumonic → complicated parapneumonic → empyema
Exudative → fibrinopurulent → organizing stages
Most common cause: complication of bacterial pneumonia
MIST2 trial: intrapleural tPA + dornase alfa improves drainage and reduces surgical need
If chest tube drainage inadequate after 24-48h, consider VATS
description: A collection of pus in the pleural space between the lung and chest wall, usually occurring as a complication of pneumonia.

name: Croup
signs:
Barking/seal-like cough
Inspiratory stridor
Hoarseness
Low-grade fever
Runny nose (prodrome)
Worse at night
Subglottic narrowing (steeple sign on XR)
Respiratory distress in severe cases
treatment:
1. Single dose dexamethasone for all patients
2. Nebulized epinephrine for moderate-severe stridor at rest
3. Observe 2-4 hours after epinephrine (rebound possible)
4. Cool mist (traditional, limited evidence)
5. Minimize agitation
6. Hospitalize if persistent stridor at rest or respiratory distress
meds:
Dexamethasone 0.6mg/kg PO/IM x1 (max 10mg)
Nebulized racemic epinephrine 0.5mL of 2.25% in 3mL NS
Nebulized L-epinephrine 0.5mL/kg of 1:1000 (max 5mL)
Prednisolone 1-2mg/kg if dexamethasone unavailable
Budesonide 2mg nebulized (alternative to dexamethasone)
ddx: Epiglottitis, Foreign body aspiration, Bacterial tracheitis, Retropharyngeal abscess, Peritonsillar abscess, Angioedema
labs:
Clinical diagnosis (labs rarely needed)
AP neck XR: steeple sign (subglottic narrowing)
Pulse oximetry
Avoid throat examination if epiglottitis suspected
Viral testing (parainfluenza) rarely changes management
notes:
Most common cause: parainfluenza virus (types 1 and 3)
Age 6 months - 3 years most affected, peak in fall
Westley croup score: stridor, retractions, air entry, cyanosis, consciousness
Single dose dexamethasone is the most important intervention
Recurrent croup: consider subglottic stenosis, hemangioma
description: A common childhood respiratory infection that causes swelling around the vocal cords, resulting in a distinctive barking cough and noisy breathing.

name: Acute Bronchitis
signs:
Cough (productive or dry) lasting 1-3 weeks
Low-grade fever
Malaise
Rhonchi or wheezing
Chest discomfort with coughing
Sore throat
Rhinorrhea
No consolidation on exam
treatment:
1. Supportive care (rest, hydration)
2. Avoid antibiotics (viral in >90% of cases)
3. Cough suppressants if needed for sleep
4. Inhaler for bronchospasm
5. NSAIDs or acetaminophen for fever/malaise
6. Honey for cough (adults and children >1yr)
meds:
Dextromethorphan 10-20mg PO q4h PRN
Guaifenesin 200-400mg PO q4h (expectorant)
Albuterol MDI 2 puffs q4-6h PRN (if wheezing)
Ibuprofen 400mg PO q6h PRN
Honey 1-2 tsp PRN (cough suppressant)
Antibiotics NOT recommended routinely
ddx: Pneumonia, Asthma exacerbation, COPD exacerbation, Pertussis, Post-nasal drip, GERD, Influenza
labs:
Usually no workup needed
CXR only if pneumonia suspected (high fever, tachycardia, focal findings)
Influenza rapid test if within 48h of symptom onset
Pertussis PCR if cough >2 weeks
ProCalcitonin may guide antibiotic decision
notes:
Viral cause >90%: rhinovirus, influenza, coronavirus, RSV
Antibiotic stewardship: acute bronchitis is #1 reason for inappropriate antibiotic prescribing
Cough may persist 2-3 weeks (post-infectious cough)
No evidence for routine use of cough suppressants, mucolytics, or bronchodilators
Consider pertussis if cough >2 weeks with paroxysms
description: A self-limited inflammation of the large airways in the lungs, usually caused by a viral infection, resulting in a cough that typically resolves within a few weeks.

name: Hemoptysis
signs:
Coughing up blood or blood-tinged sputum
Dyspnea
Chest pain
History of lung disease
Weight loss (malignancy)
Fever (infection)
Massive hemoptysis: >500mL/24h or hemodynamic instability
treatment:
1. Assess severity and hemodynamic stability
2. Protect airway (affected side down if massive)
3. Bronchoscopy for localization and potential therapy
4. Bronchial artery embolization for massive hemoptysis
5. CT angiography to identify bleeding source
6. Surgical resection (last resort for uncontrollable bleeding)
7. Treat underlying cause
meds:
Tranexamic acid 1g IV or 500mg nebulized
Cough suppressants (codeine 15-30mg PO) to reduce shearing
IV fluids and blood products as needed
Antibiotics if infectious cause
Treat coagulopathy: vitamin K, FFP, platelets
ddx: Bronchitis, Bronchiectasis, Lung cancer, TB, Pulmonary embolism, AVM, Goodpasture syndrome, Granulomatosis with polyangiitis, Mitral stenosis
labs:
CXR
CT chest (with contrast/CTA if massive)
CBC, coagulation studies, type and screen
BMP, BUN (Goodpasture)
Sputum AFB, culture, cytology
Bronchoscopy (diagnostic and therapeutic)
Anti-GBM antibodies, ANCA if diffuse alveolar hemorrhage suspected
UA (pulmonary-renal syndrome)
notes:
Massive hemoptysis (>500mL/24h): life-threatening, mortality >50% if untreated
Most common causes: bronchitis, bronchiectasis, lung cancer, TB
Bronchial arteries (systemic pressure) cause 90% of massive hemoptysis
Position patient with bleeding side down to protect unaffected lung
Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage: bilateral infiltrates + anemia + hemoptysis (Goodpasture, vasculitis, SLE)
description: Coughing up blood from the respiratory tract, which can range from blood-streaked sputum to life-threatening massive bleeding requiring emergency intervention.
